---
title: "Start Here"
---

## Why this documentation exists

Most graduate students do not struggle because the science is impossible. They struggle because the workflow is fragile: files live on laptops, results are copied by hand, folders drift out of sync, and the explanation for a key decision is trapped in somebody's memory.

The goal of this site is to make the robust path the normal path.

## The working philosophy

The original presentation reduces to a few durable defaults:

- prefer remote and reproducible workflows over local and manual ones,
- keep code, not outputs, as the source of truth,
- narrate analysis with notebooks and reports,
- track change with Git and GitHub,
- and invest early in structure so later work gets cheaper.

R for Data Science makes the same argument in a more general form: for larger projects and collaboration, the lasting record of an analysis should be the scripts, because scripts plus data can recreate the environment, while an IDE session alone cannot.[^r4ds-projects]

## Fragile versus robust workflows

| Fragile workflow | Robust workflow |
|---|---|
| Lives on one machine | Lives on supported infrastructure and in version control |
| Depends on clicking and memory | Depends on scripts, notebooks, and documented steps |
| Stores only outputs | Stores the method that creates outputs |
| Breaks when paths, packages, or people change | Survives new machines, new users, and reruns |

## What "source of truth" means in practice

Treat these artifacts as the core record of a project:

1. A `README` that says what the project is and how to run it.
2. Scripts or notebooks that recreate the analysis.
3. A lockfile or environment spec such as `renv.lock`, `environment.yml`, or `requirements.txt`.
4. Data documentation that explains inputs, keys, units, and restrictions.
5. Git history that shows what changed and why.

Treat these as derived products:

- figures,
- rendered reports,
- slides,
- manuscripts,
- and intermediate outputs that can be regenerated.

## First-week checklist

1. Get cluster access and confirm you can log in through the correct FASRC pathway.
2. Create a repository for your project before the analysis becomes messy.
3. Create a minimal project scaffold with directories for code, data documentation, figures, reports, and results.
4. Build one small end-to-end example that reads data, runs one analysis step, and renders one output.
5. Write down where the authoritative data lives and what restrictions apply.
6. Push code and documentation to GitHub before asking for help.

## What the RSE is helping you do

The RSE role is not just "fix code when it breaks." It is also to help students:

- choose the correct compute environment,
- avoid avoidable copies of large or regulated data,
- standardize project layout,
- move from ad hoc scripts to reusable workflows,
- and lower the cost of collaboration across the lab.

## Common failure modes to avoid early

- Running long jobs on a laptop because it feels faster to start.
- Hard-coding absolute paths that only work for one person.
- Saving important objects only in the interactive environment.
- Renaming scripts to `final_v2_really_final`.
- Pushing data to GitHub because the `.gitignore` was never set up.
- Waiting to document the project until after the analysis is already confusing.

## Next pages

- Go to [Workflow Roadmap](workflow-roadmap.qmd) for the full lifecycle.
- Go to [HPC and Infrastructure](hpc-infrastructure.qmd) if you need help deciding where work should run.
- Go to [Projects and Reproducibility](projects-and-reproducibility.qmd) if you are setting up a new analysis repo.

## Further reading

- [R for Data Science (2e): Workflow: scripts and projects](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/workflow-scripts)
- [R for Data Science (2e): Communicate](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/communicate)
- [NSAPH Handbook: Workflow Documentation](https://nsaph.github.io/handbook/docs.html)

[^r4ds-projects]: Hadley Wickham, Mine Cetinkaya-Rundel, and Garrett Grolemund, [*R for Data Science (2e): Workflow: scripts and projects*](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/workflow-scripts).
